From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Kanpaku](/w/index.php?title=Kanpaku&redirect=no "Kanpaku"))

Types of regent in Japan

This article is about the regent of the Emperor. For the regent of a shōgun, see [Shikken](/wiki/Shikken "Shikken").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Japan_Sessyo_Flag.svg/250px-Japan_Sessyo_Flag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Japan_Sessyo_Flag.svg)

Imperial Standard of the Regent

In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the ***Sesshō*** (摂政) was a [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") who was named to act on behalf of either a child [emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") before his [coming of age](/wiki/Coming_of_age "Coming of age"), or an [empress regnant](/wiki/Josei_Tenn%C5%8D "Josei Tennō"). The ***Kampaku*** (関白) was theoretically a sort of chief advisor for the Emperor, but was in practice the title of both [first secretary](/wiki/First_Minister "First Minister") and [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") who assisted an adult emperor. The duties of the *Sesshō* and *Kampaku* were to convey to the Emperor the policies formulated by the *Sadaijin* (左大臣, [Minister of the Left](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Left "Minister of the Left")) and other senior officials of the *[Daijō-kan](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-kan "Daijō-kan")* (太政官, [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State "Council of State")), and to convey the Emperor's decisions to them. As regents of the Emperor, the *Sesshō* and *Kampaku* sometimes made decisions on behalf of the Emperor, but their positions were not defined by law and they had no specific political authority. The two titles were collectively known as ***sekkan*** (摂関), and the families that exclusively held the titles were called *sekkan-ke* (*sekkan* family).[[1]](#cite_note-tomonokai-1)

During the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185), from the middle of the 9th century, the [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan") began to marry off their daughters to the Emperor and assume the positions of *Sesshō* and *Kampaku*, thereby excluding other clans from the political centre and increasing their political power. From the 10th century, the Fujiwara clan monopolized the *Sesshō* and *Kampaku*, and at the end of the 10th century, around the time of [Fujiwara no Michinaga](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Michinaga "Fujiwara no Michinaga") and [Fujiwara no Yorimichi](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Yorimichi "Fujiwara no Yorimichi"), the power of the Fujiwara clan reached its zenith. In the mid-11th century, [Emperor Go-Sanjo](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Sanjo "Emperor Go-Sanjo") ran his own government, and the next Emperor, [Shirakawa](/wiki/Emperor_Shirakawa "Emperor Shirakawa"), abdicated to become [Cloistered Emperor](/wiki/Cloistered_Emperor "Cloistered Emperor"), beginning the [cloistered rule](/wiki/Cloistered_rule "Cloistered rule"). From then on, the cloistered rule of Cloistered Emperor took root, and the de facto Fujiwara regime, which used the positions of *Sesshō* and *Kampaku*, was over, and the *Sesshō* and *Kampaku* lost their real political power and became mere names.[[1]](#cite_note-tomonokai-1)[[2]](#cite_note-yh040124-2)[[3]](#cite_note-kotobasekk-3)

During the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") (1185–1333), when the warrior class seized power and the [Kamakura shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate") was established, the Fujiwara were divided into [Five regent houses](/wiki/Five_regent_houses "Five regent houses") (五摂家, *Go-sekke*): the [Konoe](/wiki/Konoe_family "Konoe family"), [Kujō](/wiki/Kuj%C5%8D_family "Kujō family"), [Nijō](/wiki/Nij%C5%8D_family "Nijō family"), [Ichijo](/wiki/Ichij%C5%8D_family "Ichijō family"), and [Takatsukasa](/wiki/Takatsukasa_family "Takatsukasa family") families. From then on, these five families served as *Sesshō* and *Kampaku* on a rotating basis.[[4]](#cite_note-kotobagose-4)

[Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") was the first person in history to become a *Kampaku* who was not a noble by birth; his nephew [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu") also became a *Kampaku*. Hideyoshi obtained this title, the highest position in the aristocracy, by being adopted into the Konoe family and formally becoming an aristocrat. A retired *Kampaku* was called ***Taikō*** (太閤), which came to commonly refer to Toyotomi Hideyoshi.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-asahi240923-6)

Both *sesshō* and *kampaku* were styled as *denka* or *tenga* (殿下) in historical pronunciation; translated as "(Imperial) Highness", as were Imperial princes and princesses.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In earlier times, only members of the [Imperial Family](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") could be appointed *sesshō*. The *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* reports that [Emperor Ōjin](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cjin "Emperor Ōjin") was assisted by his mother, [Empress Jingū](/wiki/Empress_Jing%C5%AB "Empress Jingū"), but it is doubtful if it is a historical fact. The first historical *sesshō* was [Prince Shōtoku](/wiki/Prince_Sh%C5%8Dtoku "Prince Shōtoku"), who assisted [Empress Suiko](/wiki/Empress_Suiko "Empress Suiko").

The [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan") was the primary holder of the *kampaku* and *sesshō* titles. More precisely, those titles were held by the [Fujiwara Hokke](/wiki/Hokke_(Fujiwara) "Hokke (Fujiwara)") (northern Fujiwara family) and its descendants, to which [Fujiwara no Yoshifusa](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Yoshifusa "Fujiwara no Yoshifusa") belonged.

In 858, Fujiwara no Yoshifusa became *sesshō*. He was the first *sesshō* not to be a member of the Imperial house. In 887, [Fujiwara no Mototsune](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Mototsune "Fujiwara no Mototsune"), the nephew and adopted son of Yoshifusa, was appointed to the newly created office of *kampaku*.

In the 12th century, there were [five families](/wiki/Five_regent_houses "Five regent houses") among the descendants of Yorimichi called *sekke*: the [Konoe family](/wiki/Konoe_family "Konoe family"), [Kujō family](/wiki/Kuj%C5%8D_family "Kujō family"), [Ichijō family](/wiki/Ichij%C5%8D_family "Ichijō family"), [Takatsukasa family](/wiki/Takatsukasa_family "Takatsukasa family") and [Nijō family](/wiki/Nij%C5%8D_family "Nijō family"). Both the Konoe and Kujō families were descendants of [Fujiwara no Yorimichi](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Yorimichi "Fujiwara no Yorimichi"), through [Fujiwara no Tadamichi](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Tadamichi "Fujiwara no Tadamichi"). The other three families were derived from either the Konoe or Kujō families. Until the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") of 1868, those five families held those title exclusively with the two exceptions of [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") and his nephew [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu").

The offices and titles of *sesshō* and *kampaku* were abolished by the declaration of the Imperial Restoration in 1868 during the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in order to reorganize the government structure. The office and title of *sesshō* was stipulated under the former [Imperial Household Law](/wiki/Imperial_Household_Law "Imperial Household Law") in 1889 and also under the new Imperial Household Law in 1948. Under these laws, the officeholder of *sesshō* is restricted to a member of the [Japanese Imperial family](/wiki/Japanese_Imperial_family "Japanese Imperial family"). [Crown Prince](/wiki/Crown_Prince "Crown Prince") [Hirohito](/wiki/Hirohito "Hirohito"), before becoming Emperor Shōwa, was *sesshō* from 1921 to 1926 for the mentally disabled [Emperor Taishō](/wiki/Emperor_Taish%C5%8D "Emperor Taishō"). He was called *sesshō-no-miya* (摂政宮, "the Prince-Regent").

The area of Taikō in Nagoya is named after the title, although it refers to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. The main street is Taikō-dōri, which is served by the subway [Taiko-dori Station](/wiki/Taiko-dori_Station "Taiko-dori Station").
